title: Stage-Centric Microbenchmark-Calibrated Latency Modeling of Sparse Attention Kernels on Blackwell GPUs
note: Contract layer faithfully re-derived from on-board microbenchmark results (experiments/microbench/stage_model_microbench_2kernel_results.json): result_table (A1-A8 + main row) and the evidence_view mirror rewritten to the on-board diction — MAPE 3.27% over 104 configs (small 3.84% / regular 2.60%), roofline 47.6% (~14x), bottleneck = tensor-core QK^T matmul (not gather/memory), s_kv-invariance and residual recomputed from the 104 per-config rows, source_paths re-pointed to on-board artifacts. Supersedes the earlier profiler-era contract rows and their retired analysis worktrees.

detailed_outline.title: Stage-Centric Microbenchmark-Calibrated Latency Modeling of Sparse Attention Kernels on Blackwell GPUs

paper_view.working_title: Stage-Centric, Microbenchmark-Calibrated Analytical Latency Modeling of Sparse Attention Kernels on Blackwell GPUs

story_spine.problem: Deploying sparse-attention kernels on new accelerators requires predicting whole-kernel latency before exhaustive on-device sweeps, yet the kernels are data-dependent (their work scales with a learned sparse selection, not the sequence length) and memory-irregular (key/value blocks are gathered at scattered addresses).
story_spine.gap: A naive datasheet-peak roofline mispredicts these kernels by roughly an order of magnitude (about 48% mean error, ~14x the proposed model) and offers no per-stage explanation, while opaque curve-fitting predicts numbers but is neither interpretable nor portable to a new architecture.
story_spine.method: A stage-centric analytical model that decomposes the kernel into compute, contiguous query/output IO, scattered key/value gather, and fixed pipeline overhead, with two interpretable sparse-attention extensions: a data-dependent, wave-quantized tile count driven by the top-k budget and a single on-board pipeline-synchronization anchor that captures the warp-specialized residual the isolated operators miss; all coefficients come from standalone on-board microbenchmarks or datasheets, none fit to the target latencies.
story_spine.main_result: On a current Blackwell-class GPU the model predicts whole-kernel latency with 3.27% mean absolute percentage error across 104 configurations spanning two dispatch kernels, roughly 14x below the 47.6% naive-roofline error, while emitting a per-stage breakdown that correctly attributes the bottleneck to the tensor-core matmul.
story_spine.scope_limit: Validated on the sparse-prefill forward kernel of one sparse-attention family on a single Blackwell-class architecture over 104 configurations across two dispatch kernels; cross-architecture transfer is described as a coefficient-remeasurement protocol but is not yet empirically validated on a second device, and the worst single configuration sits at 9.72%, just within the sub-10% target.

narrative_strategy.central_thesis: A closed-form stage decomposition, calibrated only from standalone on-board microbenchmarks of the kernel's own operators plus datasheet constants, predicts the whole-kernel latency of data-dependent sparse-attention kernels within sub-10% error while staying fully interpretable, because the cost is set by the discrete occupancy-wave count and the top-k selection budget, and because the dominant per-iteration cost is the kernel's tensor-core matmul together with a warp-specialized pipeline-synchronization residual supplied by a single on-board anchor -- not by datasheet peaks, the full context length, or the scattered gather a sparse kernel is named for.
narrative_strategy.central_insight: For modern sparse-attention prefill kernels, what a performance model must get right is not raw FLOP/byte peaks but (a) the data-dependent amount of work induced by sparsity (the occupancy-wave count and top-k iterations) and (b) the realistic on-board cost of the kernel's own operators measured in isolation; the surprising finding is that the scattered gather is the cheapest operator and the kernel is tensor-core bound, and that the isolated operators explain under 40% of per-iteration time -- the remainder being warp-specialized pipeline synchronization captured by one anchor.
narrative_strategy.reader_takeaway: A practitioner can build an accurate, explainable latency model for a new sparse kernel on a new accelerator by decomposing the kernel into a small set of pipeline stages and plugging in independently measured per-stage coefficients, instead of either trusting datasheet roofline or fitting an opaque regressor.

insight_ladder.statement: Across configurations spanning an 8x range of context length at fixed selection budget, measured latency is essentially flat; this teaches that the kernel cost is governed by the sparse selection budget, not the dense context length.
insight_ladder.risk: Flatness could partially reflect a measurement floor; verified by the parallel flatness of the modeled tile count and per-stage IO term.

insight_ladder.statement: Standalone on-board operator microbenchmarks show the kernel is tensor-core (QK^T matmul) bound while the scattered key/value gather is the single cheapest operator; this overturns the roofline-style memory-bandwidth intuition and teaches that the decisive coefficient is the measured tensor-core throughput, not a de-rated gather bandwidth.
insight_ladder.risk: The compute/memory boundary is anchored in standalone on-board operator measurements rather than a model-internal classification; a zero-anchor ablation shows the isolated operator costs alone explain under 40% of per-iteration time, motivating the pipeline-synchronization anchor.

insight_ladder.statement: The analytical model reaches 3.27% mean error across 104 configurations spanning two dispatch kernels while the naive datasheet roofline sits at 47.6% (~14x worse); this teaches that the accuracy gain comes from stage-level structure -- wave-quantized occupancy plus a single on-board pipeline-synchronization anchor -- rather than from tuning, since no coefficient is fit to the target latencies.
insight_ladder.risk: A single residual family (top-k=1024) sits marginally above 10%, indicating a structured term still missing; reported explicitly rather than smoothed away.

positioning.closest_neighbor: Datasheet-peak roofline modeling and prior stage-centric analytical kernel models that target dense GEMM/attention; the immediate prior art is the stage-centric analytical methodology this work specializes to data-dependent sparse attention.
positioning.novelty_boundary: The new and reusable contributions are (1) treating the sparse selection budget as the latency-determining work unit via a data-dependent tile count, and (2) replacing datasheet bandwidth with a microbenchmark-measured, de-rated gather bandwidth for the irregular key/value stream, both embedded in a closed-form, no-target-fit model.
not_claiming: Not claiming state-of-the-art absolute prediction accuracy across all attention kernels or all hardware.
Not claiming empirical cross-architecture accuracy beyond the single architecture measured here.
Not claiming the model replaces profiling for final kernel selection; it is a pre-sweep predictor.
Not claiming any speedup of the kernel itself; this is a performance-modeling contribution, not a kernel optimization.

observed_facts: Whole-kernel MAPE is 3.27% over 104 configurations across both dispatch kernels (3.84% small-core / 2.60% regular); worst single-config absolute error is 9.72%.
Naive datasheet-peak roofline MAPE is 47.56% on the same configurations (~14x worse).
Measured latency is flat (within ~1%) across an 8x context-length sweep at fixed selection budget.
Standalone on-board operator microbenchmarks show every configuration is tensor-core (QK^T matmul) bound, with the scattered key/value gather the single cheapest operator (16.2 ns).
All model coefficients are sourced from standalone on-board microbenchmarks or datasheet values; none are fit to the target latencies.
allowed_interpretations: Stage-level structure (wave-quantized occupancy from the selection-driven tile count plus a single on-board pipeline-synchronization anchor) explains most of the accuracy gain over roofline.
The selection budget, not the context length, is the latency-determining work unit for this kernel.
Per-stage decomposition provides an interpretable bottleneck attribution usable for pre-sweep planning.
must_not_claim: That the model is empirically accurate on architectures other than the one measured.
That sub-10% holds for every individual configuration (one family is at 10.8%).
That this improves kernel runtime or replaces final on-device profiling.
That the result generalizes to attention kernels outside the sparse-prefill family tested.
evidence_gaps: No second-architecture measurement to validate portability empirically.
Structured under-prediction in the top-k=1024 family not yet fully explained.
Ablation and sensitivity analyses (A4, A5, A6) still to be computed offline from existing data.

core_claims.claim: A closed-form stage-centric model calibrated only from standalone on-board microbenchmarks predicts sparse-prefill whole-kernel latency with sub-10% mean error and roughly an order of magnitude lower error than a naive datasheet roofline, with no coefficient fit to the target latencies.
core_claims.scope: Sparse-attention sparse-prefill forward kernel on one Blackwell-class GPU, 104 configurations across two dispatch kernels (small-core and regular) covering selection budgets and context-length sweeps.
core_claims.what_would_falsify_it: If matching the measured accuracy required tuning any coefficient against the target latencies, or if the model error were statistically indistinguishable from the roofline error.

core_claims.claim: The latency of the sparse-prefill kernel is governed by the top-k selection budget through a data-dependent tile count and is approximately independent of the full key/value sequence length; modeling this is what closes the gap the roofline cannot.
core_claims.scope: Same kernel and device; demonstrated across an 8x sweep of context length at fixed selection budget and across three selection budgets.
core_claims.what_would_falsify_it: Measured latency rising systematically with context length at fixed selection budget, or a sequence-length-driven tile count predicting at least as well as the top-k-driven one.

core_claims.claim: The per-stage decomposition yields an interpretable, correct bottleneck attribution -- the kernel is tensor-core (QK^T matmul) bound on every configuration while the scattered key/value gather is the cheapest operator -- and the same closed form is portable to other Blackwell-class accelerators by re-measuring its on-board coefficient table.
core_claims.scope: Attribution validated on the measured device; portability stated as a coefficient-transfer protocol, not yet empirically validated on a second device.
core_claims.what_would_falsify_it: Per-stage attribution contradicted by profiler stage timings, or the bottleneck class flipping under a measured (not datasheet) coefficient change.

method_abstraction.intuition: A warp-specialized attention kernel is a software pipeline; its steady-state latency is the max of overlapped compute and memory stages times the number of work tiles, plus fixed prologue overhead. For sparse attention the number of work tiles is set by the learned selection budget, and the memory stage runs at the measured bandwidth of an irregular gather, so plugging measured per-stage rates into this structure recovers latency without fitting.
mechanism_steps: Decompose the kernel into stages: kernel launch and pipeline fill overhead, tensor-core compute, contiguous query-load and output-store IO, and scattered key/value-block gather IO.
Compute a data-dependent tile count from the top-k selection budget divided by the per-step block size, independent of the full sequence length.

Assign each stage a coefficient measured by an independent microbenchmark or read from the datasheet: tensor-core throughput ceiling, contiguous streaming bandwidth, and a de-rated effective bandwidth for the scattered gather.

Overlap compute and IO within a tile (take the max), scale by the tile count, add fixed overhead, and emit both the scalar latency prediction and the per-stage breakdown that determines the bottleneck class.
Port to a new accelerator by re-measuring the same small coefficient table with the same microbenchmarks; no structural change to the closed form.
appendix_only_details: Exact per-architecture coefficient table and its microbenchmark provenance.
Kernel structural constants (per-step block size, head shapes) read from the kernel source.
Offline evaluation harness and config enumeration.

evaluation_plan.setting: Offline closed-form prediction of whole-kernel latency over a fixed set of measured ground-truth configurations, with mean absolute percentage error against on-device kernel timing as the headline metric.
datasets_or_benchmarks: Sparse-prefill forward kernel ground-truth latencies over 104 configurations spanning two dispatch kernels (small-core and regular) across context-length sweeps and a range of top-k selection budgets.
metrics: Achieved TFLOPS / effective HBM bandwidth cross-checks
controlled_factors: No coefficient fit to target latencies (anti-overfit contract)
Same coefficient table across all configs
Fixed kernel structural constants from source

analysis_plan.title: Roofline baseline comparison
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Is the analytical model actually better than the obvious datasheet roofline, and by how much?
analysis_plan.target_display: Main-text bar chart of per-config error: analytical model vs naive roofline
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If the gap is small or inconsistent, downgrade C1 to a tie with roofline and reframe the contribution as interpretability only.

analysis_plan.title: Per-operator on-board decomposition and bottleneck attribution
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Does the model's bottleneck attribution (tensor-core bound, gather cheapest) reflect standalone on-board operator measurements rather than a modeling artifact?
analysis_plan.target_display: Main-text stacked breakdown of compute / contiguous IO / gather IO per config
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If attribution disagrees with profiler stage timings, restrict C3 to a prediction claim and drop the interpretability claim.

analysis_plan.title: Sequence-length independence at fixed selection budget
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Is latency really driven by the selection budget rather than the context length?
analysis_plan.target_display: Main-text line plot of measured and predicted latency vs context length at fixed selection budget
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If measured latency grows with context length, replace the top-k tile count with a length-dependent term and reweight C2.

analysis_plan.title: Wave-quantization-term ablation
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: How much of the accuracy gain comes from modeling the discrete occupancy-wave count rather than a continuous occupancy proxy?
analysis_plan.target_display: Main-text ablation table: full model vs (continuous-occupancy proxy) vs (no wave term)
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If removing an extension barely changes MAPE, demote that extension to a minor refinement in the method.

analysis_plan.title: Anchor necessity: zero-anchor ablation
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Are the isolated on-board operator costs alone sufficient, or is the single warp-specialized pipeline-synchronization anchor required?
analysis_plan.target_display: Ablation: predicted vs measured per-iteration time with and without the pipeline-synchronization anchor
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If MAPE is highly sensitive within the measured band, flag the coefficient as the dominant uncertainty source.

analysis_plan.title: Residual analysis of the marginal selection-budget family
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Why does the top-k=1024 family sit at ~10.8% with consistent same-sign under-prediction?
analysis_plan.target_display: Appendix residual table and candidate missing-term discussion (producer-consumer overlap / gather de-rating refinement)
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If the residual is unstructured, attribute it to measurement noise and keep the headline claim; if structured, propose the missing stage term as future work.

analysis_plan.title: Operator-layer bound classification across the config space
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Is the kernel compute (tensor-core) bound across shape families and budgets, or does the classification flip by regime?
analysis_plan.target_display: Appendix table of predicted bound class and compute/IO ratio per config
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If some shape families flip to memory-bound, scope the tensor-core-bound attribution to the measured regime and report the boundary explicitly.

analysis_plan.title: Cross-architecture portability protocol
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Can the same closed form transfer to another Blackwell-class accelerator, and what exactly must be re-measured?
analysis_plan.target_display: Appendix coefficient-transfer protocol with the minimal microbenchmark set required per architecture
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: Because no second device is measured here, this stays a protocol plus explicit limitation rather than an empirical transfer result.

reviewer_objections.objection: Ten configurations on one device is too small to support a general accuracy claim.
The good accuracy could be hidden curve-fitting rather than genuine first-principles modeling.
The cross-platform portability claim is asserted but never demonstrated on a second architecture.
The model only beats the weakest possible baseline (naive datasheet roofline).

items: analysis:A3 across-s_kv spread max 3.25% / mean 1.48% over 128x sweep

result_table.title: Headline accuracy: stage model versus naive datasheet roofline
result_table.setup_note: Per-configuration whole-kernel latency: stage-model prediction vs naive datasheet-peak roofline on the identical 104-config grid (both dispatch kernels). No coefficient fit to target latencies.
result_table.metric_summary: whole_kernel_mape_pct=3.27; worst_config_abs_pct_err=9.72; roofline_mape_pct=47.56; roofline_worst_pct=99.1; improvement_vs_roofline_x=14.5; configs_won=104/104
result_table.result_summary: Model MAPE 3.27% versus naive datasheet roofline 47.56% over all 104 configs -> about 14x lower mean error. Worst model config 9.7% versus roofline worst 99.1% (at s_q=1, where the roofline's continuous-work assumption is most wrong). Because no constant is fit to the latencies, the gain is attributable to the wave-quantized stage structure and the measured operator costs rather than to tuning.
result_table.impact_summary: Establishes C1: the stage-centric model meets the sub-10% pre-sweep target and is ~14x more accurate than the datasheet roofline.

result_table.title: Per-operator on-board decomposition and bottleneck attribution
result_table.setup_note: Per-iteration operator costs (ns) from standalone on-board microbenchmarks for both kernels (tile::gather4 TMA, register-resident exp2 softmax, QK and SV MMA atoms), compared against measured per-iteration time.
result_table.metric_summary: qk_matmul_ns=339.9; gather_ns=16.2; softmax_ns=165.2; tensor_overlap_envelope_ns=471.5; per_iteration_ns=1220; operator_sum_share_of_periter_pct=38.9; sync_residual_share_pct=61.1; sync_residual_ns=749; binder=tensor; tensor_binds=104/104
result_table.result_summary: From standalone on-board microbenchmarks: the scattered tile::gather4 (the operation the kernel is named for) is the cheapest of the four operators at 16.2 ns/block, while the overlapped binding stage is the tensor-core QK^T matmul at 339.9 ns — the kernel is compute-bound, not gather-bound. The four operators under perfect overlap sum to only ~39% (471.5 ns) of the measured ~1220 ns per iteration; the remaining ~61% (749 ns) is the warp-specialized pipeline synchronization/dependency residual, invisible to any isolated microbenchmark and supplied by the single anchor.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C2/C3: the on-board operator measurement overturns the gather-bound expectation (tensor-core binds) and quantifies the pipeline-sync residual the anchor must supply.

result_table.title: Context-length (s_kv) invariance at fixed selection budget
result_table.setup_note: Measured and predicted whole-kernel latency versus context length s_kv at fixed selection budget, across the 104-config grid; across-s_kv spread computed per batch from the on-board measured latencies.
result_table.result_summary: At fixed selection budget, measured whole-kernel latency is essentially flat across the 128x context-length (s_kv) sweep: the worst across-s_kv spread for any batch is 3.25% and the mean is 1.48%. This matches the model's prediction that whole-kernel latency is set by the occupancy-wave count and the top-k selection budget, not by the full context length — exactly the dependence a sparse-attention kernel induces and a naive roofline misses.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C1: validates the model's structural claim that cost is wave- and selection-budget-driven, not s_kv-driven.

result_table.title: Wave-quantization-term ablation: the decisive structure
result_table.setup_note: Leave-one-term ablation of the closed-form model: full model vs a variant that omits the discrete wave term (continuous work), evaluated on the full 104-config grid.
result_table.result_summary: Removing the discrete wave term n_waves=ceil(s_q/74) — treating work as continuous in the problem size, exactly what the datasheet roofline does — degrades MAPE from 3.27% to 47.6% on this grid, worst precisely where the wave structure matters most: at s_q=1 a single resident tile leaves the device nearly empty yet pays a full single-wave latency (roofline errs 99%, wave model 1.6%). Recovering the discrete occupancy-wave staircase is responsible for the bulk of the ~14x accuracy gain.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C1: isolates wave quantization as the single structural ingredient separating the model from the roofline.

result_table.title: Anchor necessity: operator costs alone are insufficient (zero-anchor ablation)
result_table.setup_note: Zero-anchor ablation of the calibration: fully-overlapped operator sum and fully-serialized operator sum (no pipeline-sync term) vs the single-anchor model, on the full 104-config grid. Supersedes the obsolete profiler-era 'per-wave-span coefficient sensitivity' analysis: the on-board model replaces the fitted per-wave span with one measured anchor, so the relevant calibration-sensitivity question is anchor necessity, not span scaling.
result_table.result_summary: Two zero-anchor bottom-up variants that sum measured operator costs over the k iterations reach only 60.5% MAPE (fully overlapped) and 44.6% (fully serialized) — both worse than the 47.6% roofline they were meant to beat — because both omit the dominant pipeline-synchronization residual. Adding the single measured single-row anchor that supplies that residual brings the model to 3.27%. Accuracy comes from composing measured operator scaling with one measured pipeline residual under the wave-quantized structure, not from operators alone nor the anchor alone.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C2: quantifies why a bottom-up operator sum is insufficient and why exactly one on-board anchor is required.

result_table.title: Residual analysis of worst-error configurations
result_table.setup_note: Per-configuration absolute percentage error decomposed by wave occupancy (num_waves) and fill, computed from the on-board 104-config grid; residual sign taken as pred minus measured on the worst single-wave configs.
result_table.result_summary: The largest residuals (~6-10%) are the partially filled single-wave configurations (s_q=32,64,74) and they share a sign: the model under-predicts the high-fill end of the first wave, because every wave is modeled with a constant cost anchored at s_q=1 whereas a nearly-full wave takes slightly longer as more tiles contend for the gather and tensor pipelines. The effect is confined to the first wave (every four-wave configuration is under 3.13%) and is sign-consistent rather than random, pointing to a mild fill response a future refinement could add without fitting target latencies.
result_table.impact_summary: Strengthens transparency: the residual is structured, interpretable, and reported openly rather than absorbed into a fitted constant.

result_table.title: Operator-layer bound classification: compute-bound, not memory/gather-bound
result_table.setup_note: Per-configuration bound classification derived from the standalone on-board operator costs: which operator forms the overlap envelope (binder) for each kernel across the 104-config grid.
result_table.result_summary: Operator-layer bound classification: the overlap envelope is the tensor-core QK^T/SV matmul for both kernels (binder=tensor on 104/104 configs), with the scattered gather an order of magnitude cheaper (16.2 ns vs 339.9 ns). The kernel is compute-bound at the operator layer, correcting the naive expectation — and a datasheet memory ceiling — that a sparse-gather kernel must be memory- or gather-bound.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C3: corrects the bottleneck attribution from memory/gather-bound (profiler-era) to tensor-core compute-bound (on-board).

result_table.title: Cross-architecture portability protocol
result_table.setup_note: Specification of exactly what must be re-measured (4 operators + 1 anchor per kernel) and what is read from source (structural constants) to transfer the model to another Blackwell-class accelerator. No second device validated.
result_table.result_summary: Portability protocol: to transfer the closed form to another Blackwell-class accelerator, re-measure the four isolated operators (tile::gather4, exp2 softmax, QK and SV MMA atoms) plus one single-row anchor per kernel; the structural constants (tiles_per_wave = num_SM/2 = 74 on the 148-SM B200, KV block size, per-iteration atom counts) are read from kernel source with no refit. Stated honestly: no second physical device was available, so portability is presented as a protocol specification, not a cross-device measurement.
result_table.impact_summary: Defines the reproducible recipe to re-target the model; scopes the portability claim honestly (protocol, single device).

result_table.title: Stage-centric wave-quantized, microbenchmark-calibrated latency model across both dispatch kernels — MAPE 3.27% over 104 configs (~14x better than roofline)
result_table.setup_note: B200 (sm100), container ds003-flashmla. Target: FlashMLA DSA sparse-prefill forward (sparse_attn_fwd, phase1.cuh), two-CTA cluster -> 74 two-SM tiles/wave on 148 SMs. Both dispatch kernels: small-budget (B_topk=64, topk=1024, 56 cfgs) and regular (B_topk=128, topk=2048, 48 cfgs); 104 configs over batch s_q in {1,32,64,74,128,148,256,296} x context s_kv. Ground truth = kk.bench_kineto median, clean (no-instrumentation) build. Stage cost composed purely from standalone on-board operator microbenchmarks (gather4 TMA, register-resident exp2 softmax, QK and SV MMA atoms) plus one single-row anchor per kernel; no in-kernel profiler, no constant fit to whole-kernel latencies.
result_table.metric_summary: latency_model=stage-centric-wave-quantized-dsa-microbench-2kernel; mape_pct=3.27; mape_pct_small=3.84; mape_pct_regular=2.6; worst_abs_pct_err=9.72; roofline_mape_pct=47.56; n_configs=104; bottleneck=tensor_core_QKt; improvement_vs_roofline_x=14.5
result_table.result_summary: Profiler-free, microbenchmark-calibrated stage model predicts whole-kernel latency at MAPE 3.27% over 104 configs (small-budget kernel 3.84% / regular kernel 2.60%), worst single config 9.7%, versus the naive datasheet roofline 47.6% (worst 99.1% at s_q=1) on the identical grid — about 14x lower error. No constant is fit to the target latencies; the only on-device whole-kernel datum is one single-row anchor per kernel (19.61us small / 32.28us regular). The overlapped per-iteration cost is set by the tensor-core QK^T matmul (339.9 ns), while the scattered tile::gather4 is the cheapest operator (16.2 ns/block).
result_table.impact_summary: Headline result: an interpretable, no-fit, on-board-microbenchmark-calibrated model is accurate (3.27% MAPE) and portable across the family's two dispatch kernels.

appendix_reproducibility: Main run record: experiments/main/dsa-stagepred-offline-v1/RESULT.json and RUN.md (branch run/dsa-stagepred-offline-v1).
Predictor: experiments/main/dsa_predictor.py (coefficients with per-field microbench/datasheet provenance).
B200 coefficient provenance: microbench-blackwell umma_throughput, compare_mem_throughput (ldgsts/tma2d), ldgsts_throughput (MLA scatter), tma2d_latency.

result_table.title: Headline accuracy: stage model versus naive datasheet roofline
result_table.setup_note: Per-configuration whole-kernel latency: stage-model prediction vs naive datasheet-peak roofline on the identical 104-config grid (both dispatch kernels). No coefficient fit to target latencies.
result_table.metric_summary: whole_kernel_mape_pct=3.27; worst_config_abs_pct_err=9.72; roofline_mape_pct=47.56; roofline_worst_pct=99.1; improvement_vs_roofline_x=14.5; configs_won=104/104

Worst model config 9.7% versus roofline worst 99.1% (at s_q=1, where the roofline's continuous-work assumption is most wrong). Because no constant is fit to the latencies, the gain is attributable to the wave-quantized stage structure and the measured operator costs rather than to tuning.
result_table.impact_summary: Establishes C1: the stage-centric model meets the sub-10% pre-sweep target and is ~14x more accurate than the datasheet roofline.

result_table.title: Per-operator on-board decomposition and bottleneck attribution
result_table.setup_note: Per-iteration operator costs (ns) from standalone on-board microbenchmarks for both kernels (tile::gather4 TMA, register-resident exp2 softmax, QK and SV MMA atoms), compared against measured per-iteration time.
result_table.metric_summary: qk_matmul_ns=339.9; gather_ns=16.2; softmax_ns=165.2; tensor_overlap_envelope_ns=471.5; per_iteration_ns=1220; operator_sum_share_of_periter_pct=38.9; sync_residual_share_pct=61.1; sync_residual_ns=749; binder=tensor; tensor_binds=104/104

The four operators under perfect overlap sum to only ~39% (471.5 ns) of the measured ~1220 ns per iteration; the remaining ~61% (749 ns) is the warp-specialized pipeline synchronization/dependency residual, invisible to any isolated microbenchmark and supplied by the single anchor.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C2/C3: the on-board operator measurement overturns the gather-bound expectation (tensor-core binds) and quantifies the pipeline-sync residual the anchor must supply.

result_table.title: Context-length (s_kv) invariance at fixed selection budget
result_table.setup_note: Measured and predicted whole-kernel latency versus context length s_kv at fixed selection budget, across the 104-config grid; across-s_kv spread computed per batch from the on-board measured latencies.
result_table.result_summary: At fixed selection budget, measured whole-kernel latency is essentially flat across the 128x context-length (s_kv) sweep: the worst across-s_kv spread for any batch is 3.25% and the mean is 1.48%. This matches the model's prediction that whole-kernel latency is set by the occupancy-wave count and the top-k selection budget, not by the full context length — exactly the dependence a sparse-attention kernel induces and a naive roofline misses.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C1: validates the model's structural claim that cost is wave- and selection-budget-driven, not s_kv-driven.

result_table.title: Wave-quantization-term ablation: the decisive structure
result_table.setup_note: Leave-one-term ablation of the closed-form model: full model vs a variant that omits the discrete wave term (continuous work), evaluated on the full 104-config grid.
result_table.result_summary: Removing the discrete wave term n_waves=ceil(s_q/74) — treating work as continuous in the problem size, exactly what the datasheet roofline does — degrades MAPE from 3.27% to 47.6% on this grid, worst precisely where the wave structure matters most: at s_q=1 a single resident tile leaves the device nearly empty yet pays a full single-wave latency (roofline errs 99%, wave model 1.6%). Recovering the discrete occupancy-wave staircase is responsible for the bulk of the ~14x accuracy gain.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C1: isolates wave quantization as the single structural ingredient separating the model from the roofline.

result_table.title: Anchor necessity: operator costs alone are insufficient (zero-anchor ablation)
result_table.setup_note: Zero-anchor ablation of the calibration: fully-overlapped operator sum and fully-serialized operator sum (no pipeline-sync term) vs the single-anchor model, on the full 104-config grid. Supersedes the obsolete profiler-era 'per-wave-span coefficient sensitivity' analysis: the on-board model replaces the fitted per-wave span with one measured anchor, so the relevant calibration-sensitivity question is anchor necessity, not span scaling.
result_table.result_summary: Two zero-anchor bottom-up variants that sum measured operator costs over the k iterations reach only 60.5% MAPE (fully overlapped) and 44.6% (fully serialized) — both worse than the 47.6% roofline they were meant to beat — because both omit the dominant pipeline-synchronization residual. Adding the single measured single-row anchor that supplies that residual brings the model to 3.27%. Accuracy comes from composing measured operator scaling with one measured pipeline residual under the wave-quantized structure, not from operators alone nor the anchor alone.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C2: quantifies why a bottom-up operator sum is insufficient and why exactly one on-board anchor is required.

result_table.title: Residual analysis of worst-error configurations
result_table.setup_note: Per-configuration absolute percentage error decomposed by wave occupancy (num_waves) and fill, computed from the on-board 104-config grid; residual sign taken as pred minus measured on the worst single-wave configs.
result_table.result_summary: The largest residuals (~6-10%) are the partially filled single-wave configurations (s_q=32,64,74) and they share a sign: the model under-predicts the high-fill end of the first wave, because every wave is modeled with a constant cost anchored at s_q=1 whereas a nearly-full wave takes slightly longer as more tiles contend for the gather and tensor pipelines. The effect is confined to the first wave (every four-wave configuration is under 3.13%) and is sign-consistent rather than random, pointing to a mild fill response a future refinement could add without fitting target latencies.
result_table.impact_summary: Strengthens transparency: the residual is structured, interpretable, and reported openly rather than absorbed into a fitted constant.

result_table.title: Operator-layer bound classification: compute-bound, not memory/gather-bound
result_table.setup_note: Per-configuration bound classification derived from the standalone on-board operator costs: which operator forms the overlap envelope (binder) for each kernel across the 104-config grid.
result_table.result_summary: Operator-layer bound classification: the overlap envelope is the tensor-core QK^T/SV matmul for both kernels (binder=tensor on 104/104 configs), with the scattered gather an order of magnitude cheaper (16.2 ns vs 339.9 ns). The kernel is compute-bound at the operator layer, correcting the naive expectation — and a datasheet memory ceiling — that a sparse-gather kernel must be memory- or gather-bound.
result_table.impact_summary: Supports C3: corrects the bottleneck attribution from memory/gather-bound (profiler-era) to tensor-core compute-bound (on-board).

result_table.title: Cross-architecture portability protocol
result_table.setup_note: Specification of exactly what must be re-measured (4 operators + 1 anchor per kernel) and what is read from source (structural constants) to transfer the model to another Blackwell-class accelerator. No second device validated.
result_table.result_summary: Portability protocol: to transfer the closed form to another Blackwell-class accelerator, re-measure the four isolated operators (tile::gather4, exp2 softmax, QK and SV MMA atoms) plus one single-row anchor per kernel; the structural constants (tiles_per_wave = num_SM/2 = 74 on the 148-SM B200, KV block size, per-iteration atom counts) are read from kernel source with no refit. Stated honestly: no second physical device was available, so portability is presented as a protocol specification, not a cross-device measurement.
result_table.impact_summary: Defines the reproducible recipe to re-target the model; scopes the portability claim honestly (protocol, single device).

result_table.title: Stage-centric wave-quantized, microbenchmark-calibrated latency model across both dispatch kernels — MAPE 3.27% over 104 configs (~14x better than roofline)
result_table.setup_note: B200 (sm100), container ds003-flashmla. Target: FlashMLA DSA sparse-prefill forward (sparse_attn_fwd, phase1.cuh), two-CTA cluster -> 74 two-SM tiles/wave on 148 SMs. Both dispatch kernels: small-budget (B_topk=64, topk=1024, 56 cfgs) and regular (B_topk=128, topk=2048, 48 cfgs); 104 configs over batch s_q in {1,32,64,74,128,148,256,296} x context s_kv. Ground truth = kk.bench_kineto median, clean (no-instrumentation) build. Stage cost composed purely from standalone on-board operator microbenchmarks (gather4 TMA, register-resident exp2 softmax, QK and SV MMA atoms) plus one single-row anchor per kernel; no in-kernel profiler, no constant fit to whole-kernel latencies.
result_table.metric_summary: latency_model=stage-centric-wave-quantized-dsa-microbench-2kernel; mape_pct=3.27; mape_pct_small=3.84; mape_pct_regular=2.6; worst_abs_pct_err=9.72; roofline_mape_pct=47.56; n_configs=104; bottleneck=tensor_core_QKt; improvement_vs_roofline_x=14.5
result_table.result_summary: Profiler-free, microbenchmark-calibrated stage model predicts whole-kernel latency at MAPE 3.27% over 104 configs (small-budget kernel 3.84% / regular kernel 2.60%), worst single config 9.7%, versus the naive datasheet roofline 47.6% (worst 99.1% at s_q=1) on the identical grid — about 14x lower error. No constant is fit to the target latencies; the only on-device whole-kernel datum is one single-row anchor per kernel (19.61us small / 32.28us regular). The overlapped per-iteration cost is set by the tensor-core QK^T matmul (339.9 ns), while the scattered tile::gather4 is the cheapest operator (16.2 ns/block).
result_table.impact_summary: Headline result: an interpretable, no-fit, on-board-microbenchmark-calibrated model is accurate (3.27% MAPE) and portable across the family's two dispatch kernels.
